front: Compare HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3. What does each version add and what problem does it solve?
back: HTTP/1.1: text-based, one request per connection (pipelining exists but broken in practice), head-of-line blocking at TCP level, persistent connections with Keep-Alive. HTTP/2: binary framing, multiplexing (multiple streams over one TCP connection, no HOL blocking at HTTP layer), header compression (HPACK), server push (deprecated in practice), ALPN negotiation. HTTP/3: built on QUIC (UDP-based), TLS 1.3 baked in, independent stream multiplexing (no TCP HOL blocking even at transport), faster connection establishment (0-RTT handshake), better mobile performance (connection migration on IP change).
senior_nuance: HTTP/2 solves HTTP-level HOL blocking but not TCP-level: packet loss stalls all streams on the connection (TCP ensures in-order delivery). QUIC eliminates this — each stream is independent at the transport layer. HTTP/3 is now supported by ~30% of web traffic. For microservices: gRPC uses HTTP/2 internally. For CDN/load balancers: terminate HTTP/3 at the edge, HTTP/2 to origin. Server push was promising but disabled in Chrome 106 — the use cases are served better by preload headers.
quote_to_say: HTTP/2 multiplexing helps at the HTTP layer but TCP head-of-line blocking remains — HTTP/3 over QUIC solves it completely.

front: What is gRPC and when do you choose it over REST? What are the tradeoffs?
back: gRPC: RPC framework using HTTP/2 + Protocol Buffers (binary serialization). Features: strongly-typed contracts (.proto files), bidirectional streaming, code generation for multiple languages, built-in deadlines and cancellation, load balancing headers. vs REST: gRPC is faster (binary vs JSON, HTTP/2 multiplexing), type-safe at compile time, streaming-native, harder to debug (not human-readable). REST: universally supported, browser-native (fetch), JSON is debuggable with curl, stateless by design, easier firewall traversal.
senior_nuance: gRPC-Web: browser-to-gRPC via a proxy (Envoy, grpc-web) — required because browsers can't use raw HTTP/2 trailers. grpc-gateway: auto-generates REST API from .proto definitions — lets you serve both gRPC and REST from the same service. Deadlines: always set client-side deadline (ctx with timeout) — a gRPC call without deadline can block indefinitely. For internal microservices: gRPC wins on performance and type safety. For public APIs: REST/JSON wins on accessibility. GraphQL: adds a query language layer, great for frontend flexibility but complex server-side.
quote_to_say: I use gRPC for internal service-to-service communication and REST for anything public-facing or browser-native.

front: What changed in TLS 1.3 compared to TLS 1.2? Explain the handshake and 0-RTT.
back: TLS 1.3 handshake: 1-RTT (one round trip instead of TLS 1.2's 2-RTT). Client sends ClientHello with key shares; server responds with ServerHello + certificate + Finished in one flight. Removed: RSA key exchange (forward secrecy now mandatory via ECDHE), static DH, RC4, SHA-1, MD5, export ciphers. Added: 0-RTT resumption (client sends early data on first flight using pre-shared key from previous session — saves one RTT but has replay attack risk, not safe for non-idempotent requests). Mandatory forward secrecy: compromise of server's private key cannot decrypt past sessions.
senior_nuance: 0-RTT replay protection: the server cannot distinguish legitimate retries from attacker replays of early data. Only use 0-RTT for safe, idempotent requests (GET). Disable 0-RTT for POST/PUT. Certificate transparency (CT): all public certs must be logged in public CT logs — browsers reject certs not in CT logs. OCSP stapling: server attaches revocation status to the TLS handshake — eliminates client OCSP round trips. mTLS (mutual TLS): both client and server present certificates — used in service mesh (Istio) and blockchain peer auth.
quote_to_say: TLS 1.3 is mandatory forward secrecy by design — I deprecate any config that allows TLS 1.2 cipher suites without PFS.

front: Explain the DNS resolution process. What is TTL, negative caching, and how does DNS affect failover?
back: DNS resolution: client queries recursive resolver (ISP or 8.8.8.8) -> root nameserver (.) -> TLD nameserver (.com) -> authoritative nameserver (example.com) -> returns IP. TTL (Time To Live): how long resolvers cache the record. Low TTL = fast failover but more queries. High TTL = cached longer, cheaper. Negative caching: NXDOMAIN responses are cached for the SOA MINIMUM value — a misconfigured DNS record causes negative cache propagation. DNS for load balancing: multiple A records (round-robin), weighted routing (Route53), geolocation routing, health-check-based failover (Route53, Cloudflare).
senior_nuance: DNS failover is not instant even with TTL=0 — some resolvers ignore TTL, OSes cache for min(TTL, socket_timeout), and connection pooling keeps old IPs alive. For fast failover: use Anycast (single IP announced from multiple PoPs — routing protocol handles failover), or client-side health checking. Also: DNS over HTTPS (DoH) and DNS over TLS (DoT) encrypt DNS queries — important for privacy and preventing ISP interception. DNSSEC signs records cryptographically — protects against DNS cache poisoning (Kaminsky attack).
quote_to_say: Low TTL is not instant failover — I always test failover timing end-to-end including resolver caches and connection pools.

front: Compare WebSockets, SSE (Server-Sent Events), and long polling. When do you choose each?
back: Long polling: client sends request, server holds it open until data is available, then responds. Client immediately reconnects. Overhead: TCP+HTTP per message. Simple to implement, works everywhere. SSE: server pushes a stream of text events over a single HTTP connection (text/event-stream). Unidirectional (server to client). Auto-reconnect built into browser EventSource API. HTTP/2 compatible. Simple for notification feeds, dashboards. WebSocket: full-duplex bidirectional connection. Upgraded from HTTP via 101 Switching Protocols. Low latency, efficient framing. Use for: real-time chat, gaming, collaborative editing, financial tickers.
senior_nuance: SSE has a 6-connection limit per domain in HTTP/1.1 browsers (not an issue with HTTP/2 multiplexing). WebSocket connections are long-lived — track them carefully for memory leaks in the server. At scale, WebSocket servers need sticky sessions (same connection to same server) or shared state (Redis pub/sub) for broadcast. SSE is often underused: for dashboard refresh, live notifications, or streaming LLM tokens (text/event-stream is how OpenAI streams), SSE is simpler and sufficient.
quote_to_say: SSE for server-to-client push, WebSockets for bidirectional real-time — most dashboard use cases don't need WebSockets.

front: Explain L4 vs L7 load balancing. What algorithms exist and what are the tradeoffs?
back: L4 (transport): routes TCP/UDP connections based on IP+port. Fast, no TLS termination needed at LB, connection-level routing. Cannot inspect HTTP headers. L7 (application): routes HTTP requests based on headers, URL, cookies. Enables: path-based routing (/api -> service A, /web -> service B), sticky sessions (cookie-based), A/B testing, header insertion, TLS termination at LB. Algorithms: round-robin (simple, equal load assumption), least connections (routes to server with fewest active connections — good for variable request duration), IP hash (sticky sessions by IP), consistent hashing (same key always routes to same backend — good for caches). Weighted variants of each.
senior_nuance: L7 LB enables blue/green and canary deployments via weight-based routing (5% to canary, 95% to stable). AWS ALB (L7) vs NLB (L4): NLB preserves client IP, ultra-low latency, better for non-HTTP protocols; ALB supports WebSocket, gRPC, HTTP/2, path/header routing. Least connections requires shared state across LB instances — in practice, power-of-two-choices (P2C) algorithm samples two random backends and picks the least loaded — avoids coordination overhead. For gRPC: L7 LB that understands HTTP/2 frames is required for request-level load balancing; L4 only does connection-level.
quote_to_say: L4 for raw throughput and protocol agnosticism, L7 for routing intelligence — I always use L7 for HTTP microservices.
